facts.text: The MOS 6502 CPU powered systems like the NES, Commodore 64, and Atari 2600.
The Zilog Z80 processor was widely used in arcade machines and home computers.
The Motorola 68000 CPU powered the Sega Genesis and many arcade systems.
The PlayStation used a custom R3000A MIPS CPU at its core.
The Nintendo 64 used a 64-bit NEC VR4300 processor.
The SNES used a Ricoh 5A22 CPU based on the 65C816 architecture.
The Atari 2600 used the MOS 6507, a stripped-down version of the 6502.
The Sega Saturn used dual Hitachi SH-2 processors.
The Dreamcast used a Hitachi SH-4 CPU paired with a PowerVR GPU.
The Xbox used a custom Intel Pentium III processor.
The PlayStation 2 used the Emotion Engine, a custom MIPS-based CPU.
The GameCube used an IBM PowerPC Gekko processor.
The Wii reused a modified PowerPC-based Broadway CPU.
The Wii U used a tri-core IBM PowerPC Espresso CPU.
The Switch uses a custom NVIDIA Tegra X1 system-on-chip.
The NES PPU (Picture Processing Unit) handled sprite rendering and scrolling.
The SNES Mode 7 chip enabled background scaling and rotation effects.
The Atari 2600 had no frame buffer and rendered graphics line-by-line.
The PS1 GPU supported textured polygons and affine transformations.
The N64 used a Reality Coprocessor for 3D graphics acceleration.
The Neo Geo hardware was essentially an arcade system in home console form.
The Neo Geo AES and MVS shared identical hardware architecture.
The Sega Model 2 arcade board powered games like Daytona USA.
The Sega Model 3 board was one of the most powerful arcade systems of its time.
The Naomi arcade system was based on Dreamcast architecture.
The CPS-1 arcade board powered early Capcom classics like Street Fighter II.
The CPS-2 board introduced stronger encryption to prevent arcade game piracy.
The CPS-3 system powered games like Street Fighter III.
The Zilog Z80 was often used as a sound CPU in arcade systems.
Arcade machines often used multiple CPUs for graphics, sound, and game logic.
The Game Boy used a custom Sharp LR35902 CPU.
The Game Boy Color used an upgraded version of the Sharp CPU with color support.
The Game Boy Advance used a 32-bit ARM7TDMI processor.
The PSP used a MIPS R4000-based CPU architecture.
The PS Vita used a quad-core ARM Cortex-A9 CPU.
The Atari Jaguar used a multi-chip architecture with Tom and Jerry processors.
The Atari Lynx was co-developed by Epyx and Atari engineers.
The WonderSwan used a low-power NEC V30MZ CPU.
The WonderSwan Color improved display hardware over the original model.
The Neo Geo Pocket used a Toshiba TLCS-900H CPU.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color improved on the original with a full-color LCD.
The Game Gear used a Zilog Z80 CPU similar to the Master System.
The Sega Master System also used a Zilog Z80 CPU.
The NES sound chip was the Ricoh 2A03, which included audio synthesis.
The SNES used a dedicated audio DSP for advanced sound effects.
The PS3 Cell processor used a unique multi-core architecture with SPE units.
The Xbox 360 used a triple-core Xenon CPU based on IBM PowerPC.
The PS4 used a semi-custom AMD Jaguar CPU and Radeon GPU.
The PS5 uses a custom AMD Zen 2 CPU and RDNA 2 GPU.
Modern consoles use unified memory architectures for CPU and GPU efficiency.
The PlayStation 2 GPU, known as the Graphics Synthesizer, had extremely high fill-rate for its time.
The PS2 Emotion Engine included vector units for physics and geometry calculations.
The Nintendo 64 Reality Coprocessor handled both graphics and audio tasks.
The Dreamcast PowerVR GPU used tile-based rendering to optimize performance.
The Sega Saturn used two video display processors for 2D and 3D rendering.
The Atari Jaguar included a GPU called Tom and a DSP called Jerry.
The Neo Geo had dedicated sprite hardware capable of handling large on-screen objects.
Arcade boards often used separate chips for sound, video, and game logic.
The CPS-2 board used encryption chips to prevent game copying and bootlegging.
The CPS-3 system required a security cartridge for game decryption at boot.
The Sega Model 3 arcade board was capable of near-real-time 3D rendering in the 1990s.
The Sega Naomi system used interchangeable ROM boards similar to Dreamcast GD-ROMs.
The Taito Type X arcade system was based on PC hardware architecture.
The CPS-1 arcade board helped define Capcom’s golden era of arcade games.
Many arcade boards used Motorola 68000 CPUs for main processing.
The NES PPU could only display 64 sprites at once.
The SNES Mode 7 allowed flat backgrounds to simulate 3D rotation.
The Game Boy screen resolution was only 160x144 pixels.
The Game Boy Advance had a resolution of 240x160 pixels.
The PSP screen resolution was 480x272 pixels.
The PS Vita featured a 960x544 OLED display in early models.
The Nintendo DS used two separate 256x192 pixel screens.
The 3DS top screen could display stereoscopic 3D without glasses.
The Wii GPU was based on an ATI Hollywood chipset.
The GameCube GPU was called 'Flipper' and was co-developed with ATI.
The PS3 GPU was based on NVIDIA’s RSX architecture.
The Xbox 360 GPU introduced unified shader architecture.
The PS4 and Xbox One both adopted AMD-based GPU architectures.
The PS5 GPU supports ray tracing acceleration in hardware.
Modern consoles use GDDR memory shared between CPU and GPU.
The Atari 2600 used a Television Interface Adapter for video output.
The Atari 2600 required developers to manage scanlines manually.
The Sega Genesis supported hardware scrolling for smoother motion.
The Neo Geo could display 380 sprites simultaneously on screen.
The Dreamcast VMU used a low-power LCD for mini-display functions.
The Game Boy used a custom LCD with no backlight.
The Game Boy Color added a reflective color LCD panel.
The Game Gear screen was backlit, but power-hungry.
The Lynx had a wide-angle LCD display for better viewing angles.
The WonderSwan used a highly efficient low-power LCD design.
The NES audio system used 5 channels for sound synthesis.
The SNES supported sample-based audio via its SPC700 chip.
The Genesis had FM synthesis audio via the Yamaha YM2612 chip.
The PS1 included a dedicated SPU for sound processing.
The N64 had advanced audio processing for real-time 3D sound effects.
Arcade machines often used CRT monitors tuned for specific refresh rates.
Many arcade boards were designed for coin-operated durability and heat resistance.
The CPS-2 system used battery-backed encryption keys that could expire.
The Sega Model 2 board was based on Sega’s partnership with Lockheed Martin.
Arcade hardware evolution drove early innovation in 3D gaming technology.
The Sega Saturn used a separate sound processor called the Yamaha FH1.
The PlayStation GPU used a fixed 1 MB of VRAM for textures and frame buffers.
The Nintendo 64 had only 4 KB of texture cache on its RCP.
The Dreamcast supported GD-ROM discs with higher capacity than CDs.
The Xbox original used a modified Windows NT kernel internally.
The PS2 used 32 MB of system RAM, which was extremely limited even for its time.
The GameCube used proprietary mini-discs instead of standard DVDs.
The Wii Remote used infrared LEDs tracked by a sensor bar for pointing control.
The Wii U GamePad included a built-in touchscreen controller display.
The Nintendo Switch supports both handheld and docked GPU modes.
The Atari 2600 joystick port became a de facto standard for later controllers.
The Atari 800 home computer shared architecture concepts with arcade systems.
The Commodore 64 used the SID chip for advanced sound synthesis.
The SID chip is still considered one of the most iconic sound chips ever made.
The Amiga used the Paula chip for audio and DMA-based data transfer.
The Amiga’s graphics chipset supported multiple display modes simultaneously.
The ZX Spectrum used a simple framebuffer with attribute-based color limitations.
The MSX standard unified hardware across multiple manufacturers in the 1980s.
The PC Engine used HuCards instead of traditional cartridges.
The Neo Geo AES cartridges could cost several hundred dollars at launch.
The CPS-2 system stored encryption keys in suicide battery-backed memory.
The CPS-3 system required CD-ROM based game loading with security checks.
The Sega Naomi 2 added improved geometry processing over Naomi 1.
The Taito Type X systems used standard PC GPUs for arcade flexibility.
The Atomiswave system was based on Sega Dreamcast architecture.
The Nintendo DS used ARM9 and ARM7 dual processors for split workloads.
The 3DS added a dedicated GPU for stereoscopic rendering.
The PSP used a Media Engine co-processor for multimedia decoding.
The PS Vita included a dedicated GPU alongside its ARM CPU.
The Game Boy Advance SP added a backlight in later revisions.
The Game Boy Color CPU ran at roughly double the speed of the original Game Boy.
The Game Gear’s color LCD made it more advanced but less power efficient than Game Boy.
The Lynx supported hardware scaling and sprite zooming in real time.
The WonderSwan’s design emphasized ultra-low power consumption.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color used a 16-bit CPU optimized for fighting games.
The Sega Dreamcast controller included a built-in VMU slot.
The VMU could function independently as a mini handheld device.
The NES Zapper used light sensing to detect CRT screen flashes.
The SNES Super FX chip enabled early 3D rendering in games like Star Fox.
The Sega Genesis had an optional Sega CD add-on for expanded storage.
The Sega 32X add-on attempted to bridge Genesis and 32-bit generation.
The Philips CD-i was an early multimedia console with CD-based software.
The Virtual Boy used red LED displays for stereoscopic 3D effects.
The Vectrex used a vector display instead of raster graphics.
The Intellivision was a competitor to the Atari 2600 with superior graphics.
The ColecoVision brought arcade-quality graphics to home consoles in the 1980s.
The Magnavox Odyssey was the first commercial home video game console.
The Fairchild Channel F was the first console to use programmable cartridges.
The Bally Astrocade featured advanced color graphics for its era.
Early consoles often relied on custom hardware rather than standardized CPUs.
The PlayStation 2 had built-in support for DVD playback without needing additional hardware.
The PS2’s Vector Units were heavily used for animation and physics calculations.
The Nintendo GameCube used a custom optical disc format to discourage piracy.
The Wii’s Broadway CPU was derived from the GameCube’s architecture.
The Wii U struggled due to limited third-party hardware optimization.
The Nintendo Switch Joy-Cons include HD rumble for advanced haptic feedback.
The Switch uses a hybrid architecture enabling docked and handheld performance scaling.
The Xbox original supported built-in hard drives, uncommon at the time.
The Xbox 360 introduced detachable hard drives for storage expansion.
The PS3’s Cell processor was extremely powerful but difficult to develop for.
The PSP UMD format was unique to Sony handhelds and never widely adopted elsewhere.
The PS Vita supported both physical cartridges and digital downloads.
The Nintendo DS Lite improved screen brightness dramatically over the original DS.
The 3DS’s autostereoscopic 3D effect required precise viewing angles.
The Game Boy Advance SP was the first Nintendo handheld with a built-in rechargeable battery.
The Game Boy line used link cables for multiplayer connectivity.
The Game Boy Color allowed limited palette changes for original Game Boy games.
The Game Gear TV Tuner allowed users to watch analog television on the device.
The Atari Lynx supported linking up to 17 devices in multiplayer sessions.
The WonderSwan had an incredibly long battery life due to efficient hardware design.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color had a micro-switched joystick for precise input.
The Neo Geo AES used identical hardware to arcade MVS systems.
The Sega Genesis had expansion ports for accessories like the Sega CD.
The Sega CD added full-motion video capabilities to Genesis games.
The Sega 32X was designed to extend Genesis life into 32-bit gaming.
The Atari 2600 joystick became one of the most recognizable controller designs ever.
The Atari 5200 controller used analog input but was notoriously unreliable.
The Intellivision controller used a numeric keypad instead of a joystick.
The ColecoVision expansion module allowed Atari 2600 compatibility.
The Vectrex included a built-in vector monitor, eliminating the need for a TV.
The Philips CD-i supported multimedia applications beyond gaming.
The Virtual Boy required a stand and viewed games through dual mirrors.
The Sega Dreamcast supported broadband modem accessories in some regions.
The PS1 memory card stored only 1 MB of save data.
The Nintendo 64 controller introduced a central analog stick design.
The SNES Super Game Boy allowed Game Boy games to be played on TV.
The NES Advantage controller was designed for arcade-style gameplay.
The Sega Master System had a light gun accessory called the Light Phaser.
The Atari Jaguar CD add-on extended storage capacity for larger games.
The Neo Geo memory card allowed arcade progress to be saved at home.
The Game Boy Camera included built-in mini-games and editing tools.
The PSP Go removed the UMD drive in favor of digital downloads.
The PS Vita Slim improved ergonomics and reduced weight.
The Nintendo DS could download demos and content via Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection.
The 3DS eShop allowed digital distribution of indie and retro games.
The Xbox Kinect used infrared depth sensing for motion tracking.
The PlayStation Move used motion tracking with colored orb controllers.
The Wii Balance Board measured weight distribution for fitness games.
Arcade cabinets often included DIP switches to configure difficulty and settings.
Hardware evolution in gaming consistently pushed graphics and memory limits forward.
